metadata.name: Groaner Jokes
metadata.description: 120 puns and dad jokes — 20 per category — where the conductor reads the setup and players must deliver the punchline without laughing. The more painful the groan, the better.
metadata.author: Trivial World Team

questions.questionText: What do you call a bear with no teeth?
questions.tidbits: "Gummy" means toothless, but gummy bears are also the beloved gelatin candy. This bear lost its bite and accidentally became a snack.

questions.questionText: Why did the scarecrow win an award?
questions.answerText: Because he was outstanding in his field!
questions.tidbits: "Outstanding in his field" is a compliment for excellence — but a scarecrow also literally stands out in a farmer's field. He never moved and somehow still won.

questions.questionText: What do you call a fish without eyes?
questions.tidbits: Spell "fish" and remove the letter I — you get "fsh." No eyes, no I. It is the most literally correct pun in marine biology.

questions.questionText: Why can't you play poker in the jungle?
questions.tidbits: "Cheetahs" sounds exactly like "cheaters." Cheetahs also live on savannas, not jungles — so there are two mistakes happening here and nobody wins.

questions.questionText: What did the Pacific Ocean say to the Atlantic?
questions.tidbits: Ocean water makes waves, and waving is also how you greet someone. The Pacific is an introvert who communicates through tides.

questions.questionText: What do you call an alligator in a vest?
questions.tidbits: Sound it out: "in-vest-igator." Put a vest on an alligator and it legally becomes a detective. The badge was already implied.

questions.questionText: What kind of shoes do frogs wear?
questions.tidbits: "Open-toed sandals" becomes "open-toad sandals." Frogs are related to toads, and apparently they prefer the breezy footwear option.

questions.questionText: What do you call a sleeping dinosaur?
questions.tidbits: "Dinosaur" plus "snore" equals "dino-snore." Scientists have not found evidence that dinosaurs snored, but this theory is just as well-researched as some others.

questions.questionText: Why don't elephants use computers?
questions.answerText: Because they're afraid of the mouse!
questions.tidbits: Elephants are popularly (if incorrectly) said to fear mice. The computer mouse gave them an entirely new reason to avoid technology.

questions.questionText: Why did the tree go to the dentist?
questions.tidbits: Trees have roots that grow underground, and a root canal is a dental procedure. The tree was in deep pain — specifically, root deep.

questions.questionText: Why don't sharks eat clowns?
questions.tidbits: "Taste funny" means an unusual flavor — but "funny" also means amusing. The shark took one bite, laughed, and let the clown go.

questions.questionText: What do you call a snowman with a six-pack?
questions.tidbits: "Abominable Snowman" (the Yeti legend) becomes "abdominal snowman." This one started working out in January and never stopped.

questions.questionText: Why did the bicycle fall over?
questions.tidbits: "Two-tired" sounds like "too tired." A bicycle has two tires, so it is literally two-tired and emotionally exhausted.

questions.questionText: What do you call a wet bear?
questions.tidbits: "Grizzly bear" becomes "drizzly bear." A drizzle is light rain. This bear got caught in a shower and is having a very bad Monday.

questions.questionText: Why do cows wear bells?
questions.tidbits: A horn is both a musical instrument and the protrusion on a cow's head. The horns are built-in but apparently defective, so they switched to bells.

questions.questionText: What do you call a dog that does magic tricks?
questions.tidbits: "Labrador" plus "abracadabra" equals "Labra-cadabra-dor." The most talented retriever in the kennel. It makes your socks disappear permanently.

questions.questionText: What do you call a cold wolf?
questions.tidbits: "Chilly dog" sounds like "chili dog" — the hot dog topped with chili. This wolf is shivering and craving something warm, ironically.

questions.questionText: Why do birds fly south for winter?
questions.answerText: Because it's too far to walk!
questions.tidbits: The expected answer is "because it's warmer" — but the punchline is pure logistics. Wings are more efficient than legs over 2,000 miles, and the birds know it.

questions.questionText: What do you call a pile of cats?
questions.tidbits: "Mountain" becomes "meow-tain." Stack enough cats and you have geographic features. The elevation is very fluffy.

questions.questionText: Why don't mountains ever get cold?
questions.tidbits: Mountains wear "snow caps" on their peaks, and a cap is clothing that keeps your head warm. They have been dressed for winter since the Ice Age.

questions.questionText: Why did the actor fall through the floor?
questions.tidbits: "It's just a stage" is what parents say about a child going through a phase. A stage is also the platform actors perform on. He fell through both meanings.

questions.questionText: What do celebrities eat for breakfast?
questions.tidbits: "Corn Flakes" becomes "Fame-flakes." A high-fiber cereal fortified with attention and endorsements. Part of a balanced ego.

questions.questionText: Why did the movie director go broke?
questions.tidbits: "Lost the plot" means going off the rails — but a director who literally loses the screenplay also loses the plot of their film. Doubly bankrupt.

questions.questionText: Why did the DJ break up with the playlist?
questions.answerText: They weren't on the same track!
questions.tidbits: "Not on the same track" means disagreeing — but a track is also a song on a playlist. The relationship had fundamental musical incompatibility from the start.

questions.questionText: What's a movie villain's favorite button on the remote?
questions.answerText: Pause — it gives him time to monologue!
questions.tidbits: Villains are famously known for long speeches right before the hero escapes. The pause button enables their worst habit. Heroes always use the time to escape.

questions.questionText: Why did the pop star go to school?
questions.tidbits: Musical notes and notes on a test paper — the star was failing both academically and vocally. The reviews were brutal. She transferred to online learning.

questions.questionText: What do you call a comedian who tells jokes about pizza?
questions.tidbits: "Cheesy" means corny or low-quality humor — but cheese is also a pizza topping. The jokes were delivered at exactly the right temperature.

questions.questionText: Why did the influencer sit in the dark?
questions.answerText: She didn't want any more followers!
questions.tidbits: "Followers" are social media subscribers — but if someone is following you in the dark, it's a very different situation. She turned off the lights and the notifications.

questions.questionText: What kind of music do balloons like?
questions.tidbits: Balloons pop when they burst, and pop is also a music genre. This is either a concert or a disaster — and the difference is only a sharp object away.

questions.questionText: Why can't country singers play hide and seek?
questions.answerText: Because good luck is always running!
questions.tidbits: Country songs are full of trucks leaving, luck running out, and things escaping. The game ends before it starts — someone's already left in a pickup.

questions.questionText: What do you call a fish who stars in movies?
questions.tidbits: Starfish (sea stars) are real marine animals — but a "star fish" is also a fish who is a movie star. Hollywood has dramatically expanded its casting.

questions.questionText: Why did the music teacher break her pencil?
questions.answerText: She didn't want to take any more notes!
questions.tidbits: Taking notes means writing things down. Musical notes are the sounds in a song. She gave up on both simultaneously and had a very productive afternoon.

questions.questionText: Why did the phone go to therapy?
questions.tidbits: A "hang-up" is a personal anxiety or fixation. It is also what you do when you end a phone call. The phone was deeply troubled and chronically disconnected.

questions.questionText: What do you call a ghost who hosts a podcast?
questions.tidbits: "Broadcaster" becomes "boo-caster." Ghosts say "boo," and this one has found a very niche audience in the paranormal podcast community.

questions.questionText: Why do actors make terrible spies?
questions.tidbits: Spies must maintain their cover identity at all times. Actors "break character" when they ruin a scene. This spy blew his cover laughing at the wrong moment.

questions.questionText: What did the streaming service say to the remote control?
questions.answerText: You really know how to press my buttons!
questions.tidbits: Pressing buttons controls a remote — but "pressing someone's buttons" also means provoking or annoying them. Their relationship is complicated and fully buffered.

questions.questionText: Why don't movies ever get cold?
questions.answerText: Because they always have a good cast!
questions.tidbits: A "cast" is the group of actors in a film — but a cast can also mean the plaster wrap on a broken limb that keeps you warm. The film was well-wrapped.

questions.questionText: What do you call a sleeping musician?
questions.tidbits: A rest in music notation is a period of silence — and resting means sleeping. This musician is completely in the zone and technically still performing.

questions.questionText: Why did the YouTube video break up with its thumbnail?
questions.tidbits: "Clickbait" refers to deceptive thumbnails or titles designed to get clicks — also, "bait" in fishing is what lures a catch. The thumbnail lost its appeal and its hook.

questions.questionText: What do you call a famous cat online?
questions.tidbits: "Influencer" becomes "mew-fluencer." "Mew" is the sound a cat makes. This feline has 4 million followers and a skincare sponsorship deal.

questions.questionText: Why don't mummies go on vacation?
questions.tidbits: Mummies are wrapped in bandages, so "unwinding" literally means their wrappings come off. It also means relaxing. Both outcomes are irreversible.

questions.questionText: What do you call a Roman emperor with a cold?
questions.tidbits: "Julius Caesar" becomes "Julius Sneezer." He conquered most of Europe but a common cold sent him to bed. Et tu, tissues?

questions.questionText: Why did Julius Caesar cross the road?
questions.answerText: To get to the other Gaul!
questions.tidbits: "Side" becomes "Gaul" — the ancient region Caesar famously divided into three parts. He always knew exactly where he was going. And why.

questions.questionText: What did Cleopatra say when she lost her makeup?
questions.tidbits: "In denial" becomes "in de-Nile." Cleopatra ruled Egypt where the Nile River flows. She refused to believe her eyeliner was gone and simply applied more.

questions.questionText: What do you call a sleeping Viking?
questions.tidbits: "Norse" refers to Viking culture, and a "napper" is someone who takes naps. This Viking went on a raid and took a rest instead. Still counts.

questions.questionText: Why didn't the caveman get the joke?
questions.answerText: It went over his Cro-Magnon head!
questions.tidbits: "Over your head" means you didn't understand something. Cro-Magnon humans were early Homo sapiens. The comedy of 30,000 BCE was admittedly ahead of its time.

questions.questionText: What do you call a famous archaeologist's dog?
questions.tidbits: "Indiana Jones" is the fictional archaeologist — "Bones" replaces "Jones." Dogs have bones and love to dig. This dog has a very fulfilling career.

questions.questionText: What did the ancient Greek say after his workout?
questions.tidbits: "Hercules" plus "I'm done" equals "Hercul-done." Hercules was the strongest hero in Greek mythology, and even he needed to cool down after leg day.

questions.questionText: Why did Napoleon always carry a pen?
questions.answerText: In case he needed to draw a retreat!
questions.tidbits: "Draw a retreat" — ordering a military withdrawal and sketching a diagram. Napoleon had a habit of tactical retreats. He was prepared for both situations.

questions.questionText: Why did the Viking refuse to fight?
questions.answerText: He didn't have the Odin-ary courage!
questions.tidbits: "Ordinary" becomes "Odin-ary." Odin is the chief Norse god. Lacking basic bravery is extremely embarrassing in Viking culture. He was reassigned to the longship kitchen.

questions.questionText: What did the Trojan Horse say to the city gates?
questions.answerText: I'm just horsing around — open up!
questions.tidbits: The Trojan Horse was the famous wooden horse full of Greek soldiers. "Horsing around" means joking. Troy really should have questioned the gift more carefully.

questions.questionText: Why was the medieval knight always tired?
questions.answerText: Because he had heavy metal fatigue!
questions.tidbits: "Heavy metal fatigue" is a real metallurgy term for when metal weakens under stress. Knights wore very heavy armor all day. The genre joke is a bonus.

questions.questionText: Why did the ancient Roman go to school?
questions.answerText: He wanted to be better versed in Latin!
questions.tidbits: "Well-versed" means knowledgeable. Latin verse is Roman poetry. He enrolled to study his own language, which seemed redundant but the grades were excellent.

questions.questionText: What's a pirate's favorite letter?
questions.answerText: You'd think it's R, but his true love is the C!
questions.tidbits: Pirates famously say "Arrr!" — suggesting the letter R. But a pirate's true love is the "C" (sea). They have sailed it their entire career. The letter R was jealous.

questions.questionText: Why did the ancient Egyptians build pyramids?
questions.tidbits: Pyramids have a specific triangular profile with deep architectural purpose — but the punchline suggests the builders just hadn't considered other shapes. Ancient constraints.

questions.questionText: What do you call a Roman who skips leg day?
questions.tidbits: "Julius Caesar" meets the legendary monster Godzilla via "quads" (quadriceps muscles). He conquered the ancient world but avoided the squat rack. History judges him.

questions.questionText: Why did the Pharaoh see a therapist?
questions.tidbits: Pharaohs are buried in elaborate tombs with all their possessions. "Burying your feelings" means suppressing emotions. He had layers of both, sealed under 3,000 years of rock.

questions.questionText: Why was the medieval sword always happy?
questions.answerText: Because it had a sharp wit!
questions.tidbits: A "sharp wit" means quick, clever humor — and a sword is literally sharp. This blade had excellent comedic timing and a very pointy delivery.

questions.questionText: Why did the Greek philosopher cross the road?
questions.answerText: To examine life on the other side!
questions.tidbits: Socrates said "the unexamined life is not worth living." Crossing the road to examine the other side is both a logical response and the least efficient form of philosophy.

questions.questionText: What do you call a Norse god who loves coffee?
questions.tidbits: "Thor" plus "thermidor" (a high-heat cooking method) plus "roast" (a coffee preparation). Thor wields lightning, which is basically high-heat — so the roast level checks out.

questions.questionText: Why did the artist go to jail?
questions.tidbits: "Being framed" means being set up for a crime you didn't commit — but paintings are also put in frames. The innocent canvas said nothing at trial.

questions.questionText: Why did the cartoonist get fired?
questions.tidbits: "Draw a conclusion" means to reach a logical end — and drawing is what cartoonists do. He was incapable of finishing either a logical argument or a comic strip.

questions.questionText: Why did Van Gogh become a painter?
questions.answerText: Because he didn't have an ear for music!
questions.tidbits: Van Gogh famously cut off part of his own ear. "No ear for music" means tone-deaf. He chose painting for entirely sensible reasons.

questions.questionText: What did one paintbrush say to the other?
questions.answerText: I really get a stroke out of this!
questions.tidbits: A brush stroke is how paint is applied — and "getting a stroke" can mean having a great time or a medical event. The brushes were enthusiastic either way.

questions.questionText: Why did the pencil break up with the eraser?
questions.answerText: Because the eraser kept rubbing it the wrong way!
questions.tidbits: "Rubbing someone the wrong way" means irritating them — and erasers work by rubbing marks away. This was an incompatible relationship from the very first line.

questions.questionText: What do you call an art thief who only steals clocks?
questions.tidbits: "Masterpiece" (a great work of art) becomes "master of timepieces." A timepiece is a clock. This criminal has very specific taste and an excellent alibi.

questions.questionText: Why did the watercolor cry?
questions.answerText: Because it couldn't hold it together!
questions.tidbits: Watercolors are known for bleeding and blending when wet — they literally cannot hold their shape. "Can't hold it together" also means falling apart emotionally. Very relatable medium.

questions.questionText: What did Picasso say when he was cold?
questions.tidbits: Picasso's Blue Period (1901–1904) featured paintings mostly in blue tones, associated with sadness. Being cold also makes you feel blue. He was miserable and incredibly productive.

questions.questionText: Why do artists make great bakers?
questions.answerText: They know how to use a glaze!
questions.tidbits: A glaze in baking is a shiny coating on pastries — a glaze in painting is a thin transparent layer of color. Artists have cross-disciplinary skills nobody expected.

questions.questionText: What do you call a sketch that tells jokes?
questions.tidbits: A "comic" is both a sketch-based cartoon strip and a comedian who tells jokes. This art form has been doing double duty all along and billing for both.

questions.questionText: Why did the art museum go out of business?
questions.tidbits: "Drawback" means a disadvantage or flaw — and drawing is what artists do. The museum was losing on every level. The gift shop was the only profitable exhibit.

questions.questionText: What do you call an animated movie about laundry?
questions.tidbits: A "spin-off" is a media franchise extension based on an original work — clothes in a washing machine also spin. This animated laundry saga reportedly has three sequels.

questions.questionText: Why did the sculptor go on a diet?
questions.answerText: She had a lot on her plate and kept chipping away at it!
questions.tidbits: "A lot on your plate" means being overwhelmed. Sculptors chip away at stone. She was managing both simultaneously with the dedication of a true artist.

questions.questionText: Why did the art student fail the test?
questions.answerText: She just couldn't get the picture!
questions.tidbits: "Get the picture" means to understand something. Understanding a painting is also what art criticism requires. She failed both the test and the gallery critique.

questions.questionText: Why are cartoons always so happy?
questions.answerText: Because they draw their own smiles!
questions.tidbits: Cartoon characters are literally drawn — including their expressions. They have complete control over their emotional state and have chosen perpetual joy. Smart.

questions.questionText: What do you call a bear in a Cubist painting?
questions.tidbits: Cubism is the famous abstract art style invented by Picasso and Braque. A bear painted in Cubism would have 47 noses from 12 different angles and be unfathomable.

questions.questionText: Why did the comic book villain become a chef?
questions.answerText: He wanted to take over the world — one dish at a time!
questions.tidbits: Classic villain world domination meets culinary ambition. The restaurant empire strategy is underrated. He started with a food truck and now controls three time zones.

questions.questionText: Why couldn't the artist finish the painting?
questions.tidbits: "Drawing a blank" means being unable to think of something — and drawing is literally what the artist does. The canvas and the mind were equally empty.

questions.questionText: What do you call a cartoonist who is also a locksmith?
questions.answerText: Someone who really draws the line!
questions.tidbits: "Drawing the line" means setting a limit or boundary — and artists draw lines as their primary activity. The locksmith side of the business handles all the key art.

questions.questionText: Why do painters always win arguments?
questions.answerText: Because they always make their point!
questions.tidbits: "Making your point" means proving your argument — and a painter uses a pointy brush to apply paint. This artist is both persuasive and well-equipped.

questions.questionText: Why did the computer go to the doctor?
questions.tidbits: Computer viruses are malicious software programs — biological viruses make you sick. The computer sneezed binary code and had to take the week off.

questions.questionText: What do you call a fish that knows how to code?
questions.tidbits: Python is one of the most popular programming languages. It is also a large snake, not a fish — but the pun works better this way. The fish graduated top of its class.

questions.questionText: What's an astronaut's favorite key on the keyboard?
questions.tidbits: The space bar is the longest key on a keyboard, used to create spaces between words — and space is where astronauts work. It is the most cosmically important key.

questions.questionText: Why don't scientists trust atoms?
questions.tidbits: Atoms are the building blocks of all matter — they literally make up everything in the universe. "Making things up" also means lying. Atoms are the universe's biggest fibbers.

questions.questionText: Why did the programmer quit his job?
questions.tidbits: "Didn't get any raises" sounds like "didn't get arrays." An array is a fundamental data structure in programming. He asked for a salary increase and received a variable instead.

questions.questionText: What did the WiFi say to the laptop?
questions.tidbits: WiFi and laptops connect wirelessly — and "feeling a connection" is what you say when you bond with someone. This is a love story measured entirely in megabits.

questions.questionText: Why did the math teacher go to the beach?
questions.tidbits: A tangent in math is a line that touches a curve at one point — going "off on a tangent" means digressing wildly. The teacher was doing both while building a sand fortress.

questions.questionText: Why was the math book always stressed?
questions.answerText: Because it had too many problems!
questions.tidbits: A math book is full of practice problems — and having "too many problems" means your life is difficult. The book sought therapy and found it had imaginary solutions.

questions.questionText: Why don't robots ever panic?
questions.tidbits: Coolant is the fluid that keeps machines from overheating. "Keeping your cool" means staying calm under pressure. Robots are chemically chilled and emotionally unflappable.

questions.questionText: Why did the biologist break up with the physicist?
questions.tidbits: "No chemistry" describes romantic incompatibility — and Chemistry is the science that sits between biology and physics. The irony was noted by everyone except them.

questions.questionText: What do you call an astronaut's favorite chocolate?
questions.tidbits: Mars bars are a popular chocolate candy. Mars is also the fourth planet from the Sun, a destination for future crewed missions. The candy is the closest most of us will get.

questions.questionText: Why did the electrician get in trouble?
questions.answerText: He was always making light of serious situations!
questions.tidbits: "Making light of" means treating something serious as a joke. Electricians literally make light by wiring circuits and installing fixtures. His humor was consistently well-lit.

questions.questionText: Why did the cell phone need glasses?
questions.tidbits: "Losing contacts" on a phone means your address book was deleted — contact lenses are worn when you have bad eyesight. The phone is now both near-sighted and friendless.

questions.questionText: Why did the astronomer get arrested?
questions.tidbits: "Mooning" someone is a slang term for showing your backside as a prank — an astronomer also studies the moon professionally. The charges were confusing for everyone involved.

questions.questionText: What did one tectonic plate say to the other when they collided?
questions.tidbits: Geological faults are the fracture zones where tectonic plates meet and cause earthquakes. "My fault" also means "I'm to blame." This is the most self-aware plate in geology.

questions.questionText: Why did the chemistry teacher take up gardening?
questions.answerText: She had a natural reaction to it!
questions.tidbits: A "natural reaction" means an instinctive response — in chemistry, reactions are the core subject. Her enthusiasm was entirely organic.

questions.questionText: What do you call a number that can't sit still?
questions.tidbits: "Roman numeral" (I, V, X, L, C) becomes "roamin' numeral." Roman numerals are an ancient numbering system — but this one just can't stop moving around the number line.

questions.questionText: Why did the sun go to school?
questions.tidbits: "Brighter" can mean more intelligent or more luminous. The sun enrolled in kindergarten and was already overqualified. It still asked great questions about nuclear fusion.

questions.questionText: What did the ocean say to the beach?
questions.answerText: Nothing. It just waved. Then sent a shore message!
questions.tidbits: Waves are the ocean's greeting, and "shore" sounds like "sure." A "shore message" replaces a "sure message." The ocean communicates exclusively through geological puns.

questions.questionText: Why did the comet get a speeding ticket?
questions.answerText: It was caught in a meteor shower!
questions.tidbits: A meteor shower is when debris burns up in Earth's atmosphere — but "shower" also suggests being doused in something. The comet was going 70,000 mph in a 65,000 mph zone.

questions.questionText: Why did the football coach go to the bank?
questions.tidbits: A quarterback is the key position in American football — and getting your "quarter back" means reclaiming 25 cents. The coach negotiated a very specific refund.

questions.questionText: What do basketball players eat for breakfast?
questions.tidbits: A three-point shot is the highest-scoring regular play in basketball — "three-point meal" replaces "three-course meal." The athlete is optimizing every aspect of game day.

questions.questionText: Why did the golfer bring extra pants?
questions.answerText: In case he got a hole in one!
questions.tidbits: A "hole in one" is when a golfer sinks the ball with a single shot — it's rare and celebrated. Pants with a hole in them are ruined. He packed spares for both outcomes.

questions.questionText: Why can't Cinderella play soccer?
questions.answerText: Because she always runs away from the ball!
questions.tidbits: Cinderella runs away from the royal ball at midnight. In soccer, staying with the ball is essential. She is constitutionally unable to commit and her cleats were glass.

questions.questionText: Why did the gym close down?
questions.tidbits: "Work out" means to exercise at the gym — it also means to succeed or resolve well. The gym failed on both counts at exactly the same time.

questions.questionText: Why did the baseball team go to the library?
questions.tidbits: "Pitching" a business idea means presenting it persuasively — a pitcher also throws the ball in baseball. The outfield is now surprisingly well-read and articulate.

questions.questionText: Why did the soccer player bring string to the game?
questions.tidbits: "Tie the score" means to equalize in a match — and tying something requires string or rope. She came prepared for both athletic and practical contingencies.

questions.questionText: What do you call a karate champion who mops floors?
questions.tidbits: "Bruce Lee" becomes "Bruce Clean." Bruce Lee is the legendary martial artist. This version has identical moves but a completely different job description and uniform.

questions.questionText: Why did the hockey player go to the dentist?
questions.answerText: He wanted to improve his face-off!
questions.tidbits: A face-off is how play starts in hockey — players face each other and the puck drops between them. The dentist assessed the teeth and agreed a better face was needed overall.

questions.questionText: What do you call a lazy athlete?
questions.tidbits: A couch potato is someone who lies on the couch doing nothing. This athlete has merged the lifestyle with the sport. They are training for the horizontal sprint.

questions.questionText: Why did the video game villain go to therapy?
questions.answerText: He had too many final boss issues!
questions.tidbits: "Boss issues" means problems with authority figures. In video games, the final boss is the ultimate obstacle. He has been defeated 500 times and has complex feelings about it.

questions.questionText: Why did the swimmer bring a towel to school?
questions.answerText: She heard the teacher was going to give a pop quiz — and she wanted to dry off from the drills!
questions.tidbits: A "pop quiz" is a surprise test — and this swimmer confused schoolwork with swim practice. She trained for both and was prepared for neither.

questions.questionText: What do you call a running back who also knits?
questions.tidbits: A "yardage gain" is when a player moves forward in football — "yard" becomes "yarn." This running back has excellent stats and is working on a very long scarf.

questions.questionText: Why don't skeletons play sports?
questions.answerText: Because they have no body to play with!
questions.tidbits: "No body to play with" sounds like "nobody to play with." Skeletons literally have no body — just bones. They were cut from the team on purely anatomical grounds.

questions.questionText: What's a swimmer's favorite subject in school?
questions.tidbits: "Politics" becomes "Pool-itics." This swimmer cares deeply about governance but only within aquatic environments. They ran for class president on a platform of better lane ropes.

questions.questionText: Why did the tennis player get sent home from school?
questions.tidbits: In tennis, a "fault" is when a serve doesn't land in the correct area — having "too many faults" in school means making too many mistakes. He was suspended from both simultaneously.

questions.questionText: What do you call a video game character who becomes a chef?
questions.tidbits: "Leveling up" is how characters grow in video games — a "level-headed" person is calm and rational. This character maxed out the cooking skill tree and opened a restaurant.

questions.questionText: Why did the basketball player go to the bank?
questions.answerText: He heard they had great interest in assists!
questions.tidbits: "Interest" is what banks charge on loans — an "assist" in basketball is when you help a teammate score. The bank confirmed they mostly prefer deposits. The player was disappointed.

questions.questionText: Why did the bowling pins go on strike?
questions.answerText: Because they were tired of being knocked around!
questions.tidbits: A "strike" in bowling is knocking all pins down in one throw — going on strike also means refusing to work as a protest. The pins organized and nobody blamed them.

questions.questionText: What do you call a sprinter who also makes coffee?
questions.tidbits: Espresso is the fast concentrated coffee shot — a sprinter is the fastest human athlete. This person gets everything done in under 10 seconds and then immediately needs another cup.
